draft.introduction: Jesus’ teaching on anxiety: “Do not be anxious about your life.” The lilies of the field and ravens illustrate God’s providential care, grounding freedom from worry in trust in the Father.

translation.text: And he said to his disciples, “Therefore I tell you, do not be anxious about your life, what you will eat, nor about your body, what you will put on. For life is more than food, and the body more than clothing. Consider the ravens: they neither sow nor reap, yet God feeds them. Consider the lilies, how they grow: they neither toil nor spin, yet I tell you, even Solomon in all his glory was not arrayed like one of these. Instead, seek his kingdom, and these things will be added to you.”
translation.original_text: μὴ μεριμνᾶτε τῇ ψυχῇ τί φάγητε

translator_notes.note: The verb μεριμνάω (“be anxious”) implies a divided mind—the same root as μερίζω (“divide”). Anxiety is presented not as a feeling to suppress but as a theological problem: a divided loyalty between God and worldly security.
